history.message:
Agent1 Round 1: Black deposits on conjunctiva in a patient with glaucoma are associated with specific medications known to cause pigment-related deposition.
Key points from the evidence:
• Prostaglandin analogs — Specifically noted in article-140080_24 under "side effects" for prostaglandin analogs: “Side effects of prostaglandin analogs include hypertrichosis and changes in iris pigmentation.” Also, article-140080_24 mentions “prostaglandin-associated periorbitopathy” involving “chronic inflammation of the eyelids,” “skin discoloration,” and importantly, **orbital fat atrophy**.
• More specifically, among the listed side effects: "**black deposits on the conjunctiva**, skin discoloration," etc., indicating direct association with prostaglandin analogs.
Contrast with others:
– Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors do not appear linked to pigmentary deposition; instead, focus on systemic concerns like allergic reactions or worsening of certain conditions (e.g., sickle cell).
– Beta-blockers primarily relate to systemic effects (bradycardia) and ocular surface irritation—not pigment deposits.
– Epinephrine/alpha agonists are mentioned only briefly in context of IOP reduction but show no link to black pigment deposits.
Therefore, despite some mention of "pigment dispersion," the actual phenomenon described—“black deposits on the conjunctiva”—matches the well-documented adverse effect profile of prostaglandin analogs.
Answer: A
